title: State Machine Design for Business Domains
frameworks: Deal Lifecycle: draft → proposed → negotiating → accepted → invoiced → paid → closed → cancelled
Commercial: prospect → qualifying → proposal → negotiation → closed_won → closed_lost
Follow-Up: scheduled → attempted → completed → overdue → cancelled
Contact: prospect → lead → active_customer → alumni → churned
Event: planned → confirmed → in_progress → completed → cancelled
concepts: Every business entity has a lifecycle that can be modeled as a state machine
Guard conditions prevent invalid transitions (e.g., cannot invoice a cancelled deal)
Terminal states are absorbing — once entered, no further transitions
State machines make implicit business rules explicit and testable
Each transition should be logged with actor, timestamp, and reason
State machines prevent the most common business logic bug: impossible states
context: Extracted from ordostudio — 7 production state machines covering deals, ledger entries, commercial pipelines, follow-ups, instructor scheduling, contacts, and events. Each machine defines explicit states, valid transitions, guard conditions, and terminal states. State machines enforce business rules that would otherwise be scattered across application code.
